title: What Are Bifocal Glasses? How They Work and How They Compare to Progressives
description: Bifocal glasses have two lens zones for distance and reading, separated by a visible line. Learn how they work, who they're for, and whether progressives are better.
tldr: Bifocal glasses have two distinct lens zones — distance on top and near on the bottom — separated by a visible line. They correct presbyopia (age-related near vision loss) and are an alternative to progressive lenses, with the trade-off of no intermediate zone and a visible line.

tldrStats.label: Vision zones in a bifocal lens

definition.text: Bifocal glasses are eyeglasses with lenses that contain two distinct optical zones: an upper area for distance vision and a lower segment for near vision (reading), separated by a visible line. Invented by Benjamin Franklin in 1784, bifocals correct presbyopia by combining distance and near prescriptions in a single lens, eliminating the need to switch between two pairs of glasses.

sections.content:
A bifocal lens is essentially two lenses fused into one. The main (larger) portion is ground to your distance prescription. A smaller segment at the bottom is set to your reading prescription.

The most common bifocal design is the flat-top (D-segment) — a half-moon shaped reading segment in the lower portion of the lens, with a straight line across the top of the segment. There are other designs (round segment, executive/Franklin style), but flat-top accounts for the vast majority of bifocals prescribed today.

Using them is intuitive: look straight ahead or up for distance, look down through the lower segment for reading. The transition is immediate — no gradual blending like progressive lenses.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

Bifocals are prescribed for people with presbyopia — the gradual loss of near focusing ability that starts around age 40. If you need different prescriptions for distance and reading, bifocals are one solution.

People who tend to prefer bifocals over progressives include:

  • Those who've worn bifocals for years and are comfortable with them
  • People who had difficulty adapting to progressive lenses (some never adjust to the peripheral blur)
  • Those who want an instant, no-adjustment-period switch to multifocal lenses
  • People with specific occupational needs where a clear, wide reading zone matters more than intermediate vision

That said, progressive lenses have become the default multifocal recommendation for most optometrists. If you're being prescribed multifocal lenses for the first time, your optometrist at Charm Optical will likely discuss both options with you.

This is the comparison most people want to know about.

Bifocal advantages:

  • No adjustment period — the two zones are immediately clear
  • Wider reading zone than most progressive lenses
  • No peripheral blur or "swimmy" feeling
  • Less expensive than progressive lenses

Bifocal disadvantages:

  • Visible line on the lens (cosmetic concern for many)
  • No intermediate zone — computer screens fall in a no-man's-land between the two zones
  • Abrupt transition when your gaze crosses the line (called "image jump")

Progressive advantages:

  • No visible line — looks like regular single vision glasses
  • Intermediate zone for computer use
  • Smooth transition between all distances

Progressive disadvantages:

  • 1-2 week adjustment period
  • Peripheral blur in the lower corners
  • Narrower reading zone than bifocals
  • More expensive

For most first-time multifocal wearers, progressives are the better starting point — they're more versatile and more modern. But bifocals remain a perfectly valid choice, especially for people who find progressives uncomfortable.

Flat-top (D-segment): The most popular design. A D-shaped reading segment with a flat top edge. Available in 28mm and 35mm segment widths. The 28mm is standard; the 35mm provides a wider reading area.

Round segment: A circular reading segment. Less common today but still available. Some people find the round shape less visually distracting.

Executive (Franklin): The entire lower half of the lens is the reading prescription — no small segment, just a line across the middle. Provides the widest possible reading area but is heavier and less cosmetically appealing.

Blended bifocal: The line between segments is softened so it's less visible. A compromise between standard bifocals and progressives, though the intermediate zone is still missing.

If you're experiencing any of these signs regularly, it's worth getting a professional assessment rather than guessing. Many vision problems develop gradually — your brain compensates for declining vision so effectively that you may not notice the change until it's significant. A comprehensive eye exam can detect issues that self-assessment misses, including early signs of conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration that have no symptoms in their early stages.

The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends eye exams every 1-2 years for adults. Children should be examined annually since vision problems can significantly impact learning and development. In Alberta, children under 18 receive coverage through Alberta Health. Adults with diabetes or a family history of eye disease should also get annual exams. Book an appointment at Charm Optical — they accept most Alberta insurance providers through direct billing.

sections.h2: How Much Do Bifocal Glasses Cost in Canada?
sections.content:
Bifocal lenses are generally less expensive than progressive lenses:

  • Basic bifocal lenses: $150-250 per pair
  • Mid-range bifocals with coatings: $250-400
  • Complete pair (frames + bifocal lenses): $300-600

Compare this to progressive lenses, which start around $250 for basic and go up to $900+ for premium designs. If budget is a primary concern and you don't need intermediate vision correction, bifocals offer real savings.

Most Alberta insurance plans cover bifocal lenses within their optical benefit. Direct billing at Charm Optical means you only pay the remaining balance after insurance.

Pricing varies significantly depending on where you buy and what lens features you choose. In Canada, the total cost of a complete pair of glasses (frame + lenses) typically breaks down as follows: budget options start at $150-$250 for basic frames with standard single vision lenses. Mid-range options with anti-reflective coating and a name-brand frame run $300-$500. Premium designer frames with progressive or high-index lenses can reach $600-$900+. The biggest variable is usually the lens — a basic single vision lens costs far less than a digital freeform progressive with premium coatings.

Insurance makes a significant difference. Most employer health plans in Alberta cover $200-$500 toward glasses every 1-2 years. Charm Optical direct-bills most Alberta insurance providers — Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, Desjardins, and more — so you only pay the difference at the counter. No paperwork, no reimbursement wait. Book an eye exam to get your prescription checked and learn exactly what your plan covers before you shop.

Frame selection for bifocals is less restrictive than for progressives, but there are still a few guidelines:

  • Adequate depth: The frame needs enough vertical height to fit both the distance zone and the reading segment comfortably. Very shallow frames won't leave enough room.
  • Segment placement: Your optician will position the bifocal line at or just below your lower pupil margin when looking straight ahead. This ensures you can see distance without the reading segment interfering, and can access the reading zone with a natural downward gaze.
  • Frame style: Because the bifocal line is visible, some people choose frames where the line is less noticeable — thicker acetate frames, for example, can draw attention away from the line compared to rimless frames where the line is fully exposed.

Your optician at Charm Optical can show you how different frame styles look with bifocal lenses and help you make a choice you'll be happy with. Book an appointment to get your prescription checked and explore your options.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: Can you still get bifocal glasses?
faqs.a: Absolutely. While progressive lenses have become more popular, bifocals are still widely available and prescribed. Many people prefer them for the wide reading zone, no adjustment period, and lower cost. Any optical store can make bifocal lenses for you.

Neither is universally better — they solve the same problem differently. Bifocals offer a wider reading zone with no adjustment period but lack intermediate vision and have a visible line. Progressives are seamless and include intermediate vision but require an adaptation period. Your optometrist at Charm Optical can help you decide which is right for your needs.

faqs.q: Can you see a computer screen with bifocals?
faqs.a: Not ideally. Computer screens sit at an intermediate distance that falls between the two bifocal zones. You may find yourself tilting your head back to use the reading segment or leaning closer to the screen. If computer use is a big part of your day, progressives or dedicated computer glasses may be better options.

faqs.q: How much do bifocals cost compared to progressives in Canada?
faqs.a: Bifocal lenses typically cost $150-400 depending on material and coatings, compared to $250-900+ for progressive lenses. A complete pair of bifocal glasses usually runs $300-600, which is $100-400 less than a comparable progressive pair.

faqs.q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
faqs.a: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.

faqs.q: How often should I replace my lenses?
faqs.a: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.
